title: Paradise Regained - The First Book
content:
آ آ آ آ I who eï؟½re while the happy Garden sung,
آ آ آ آ By one mans disobedience lost, now sing
آ آ آ آ Recoverï؟½d Paradise to all mankind,
آ آ آ آ By one mans firm obedience fully triï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Through all temptation, and the Tempter foilï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In all his wiles, defeated and repulsï؟½t,
آ آ آ آ And Eden raisï؟½d in the wast Wilderness.
آ آ آ آ Thou Spirit who ledst this glorious Eremite
آ آ آ آ Into the Desert, his Victorious Field
آ آ آ آ Against the Spiritual Foe, and broughtst him thence
آ آ آ آ By proof the undoubted Son of God, inspire,
آ آ آ آ As thou art wont, my prompted Song else mute,
آ آ آ آ And bear through highth or depth of natures bounds
آ آ آ آ With prosperous wing full summï؟½d to tell of deeds
آ آ آ آ Above Heroic, though in secret done,
آ آ آ آ And unrecorded left through many an Age,
آ آ آ آ Worthy tï؟½have not remainï؟½d so long unsung.
آ آ آ آ Now had the great Proclaimer with a voice
آ آ آ آ More awful then the sound of Trumpet, criï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Repentance, and Heavens Kingdom nigh at hand
آ آ آ آ To all Baptizï؟½d: to his great Baptism flockï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With aw the Regions round, and with them came
آ آ آ آ From Nazareth the Son of Joseph deemï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To the flood Jordan, came as then obscure,
آ آ آ آ Unmarkt, unknown; but him the Baptist soon
آ آ آ آ Descriï؟½d, divinely warnï؟½d, and witness bore
آ آ آ آ As to his worthier, and would have resignï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To him his Heavenly Office, nor was long
آ آ آ آ His witness unconfirmï؟½d: on him baptizï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Heaven openï؟½d, and in likeness of a Dove
آ آ آ آ The Spirit descended, while the Fathers voice
آ آ آ آ From Heavï؟½n pronouncï؟½d him his beloved Son.
آ آ آ آ That heard the Adversary, who roving still
آ آ آ آ About the world, at that assembly famï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Would not be last, and with the voice divine
آ آ آ آ Nigh Thunder-struck, thï؟½ exalted man, to whom
آ آ آ آ Such high attest was givï؟½n, a while surveyï؟½d
آ آ آ آ With wonder, then with envy fraught and rage
آ آ آ آ Flies to his place, nor rests, but in mid air
آ آ آ آ To Councel summons all his mighty Peers,
آ آ آ آ Within thick Clouds and dark ten-fold involvï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ A gloomy Consistory; and them amidst
آ آ آ آ With looks agast and sad he thus bespake.
آ آ آ آ O ancient Powers of Air and this wide world,
آ آ آ آ For much more willingly I mention Air,
آ آ آ آ This our old Conquest, then remember Hell
آ آ آ آ Our hated habitation; well ye know
آ آ آ آ How many Ages, as the years of men,
آ آ آ آ This Universe we have possest, and rulï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In manner at our will thï؟½ affairs of Earth,
آ آ آ آ Since Adam and his facil consort Eve
آ آ آ آ Lost Paradise deceivï؟½d by me, though since
آ آ آ آ With dread attending when that fatal wound
آ آ آ آ Shall be inflicted by the Seed of Eve
آ آ آ آ Upon my head, long the decrees of Heavï؟½n
آ آ آ آ Delay, for longest time to him is short;
آ آ آ آ And now too soon for us the circling hours
آ آ آ آ This dreaded time have compast, wherein we
آ آ آ آ Must bide the stroak of that long threatnï؟½d wound,
آ آ آ آ At least if so we can, and by the head
آ آ آ آ Broken be not intended all our power
آ آ آ آ To be infringï؟½d, our freedom and our being.
آ آ آ آ In this fair Empire won of Earth and Air;
آ آ آ آ For this ill news I bring, the Womans seed
آ آ آ آ Destinï؟½d to this, is late of woman born,
آ آ آ آ His birth to our just fear gave no small cause,
آ آ آ آ But his growth now to youths full flowr, displaying
آ آ آ آ All vertue, grace and wisdom to atchieve
آ آ آ آ Things highest, greatest, multiplies my fear.
آ آ آ آ Before him a great Prophet, to proclaim
آ آ آ آ His coming, is sent Harbinger, who all
آ آ آ آ Invites, and in the Consecrated stream
آ آ آ آ Pretends to wash off sin, and fit them so
آ آ آ آ Purified to receive him pure, or rather
آ آ آ آ To do him honour as their King; all come,
آ آ آ آ And he himself among them was baptizï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Not thence to be more pure, but to receive
آ آ آ آ The testimony of Heaven, that who he is
آ آ آ آ Thenceforth the Nations may not doubt; I saw
آ آ آ آ The Prophet do him reverence, on him rising
آ آ آ آ Out of the water, Heavï؟½n above the Clouds
آ آ آ آ Unfold her Crystal Dores, thence on his head
آ آ آ آ A perfect Dove descend, what eï؟½re it meant,
آ آ آ آ And out of Heavï؟½n the Sovï؟½raign voice I heard,
آ آ آ آ This is my Son belovï؟½d, in him am pleasï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ His Mother then is mortal, but his Sire,
آ آ آ آ He who obtains the Monarchy of Heavï؟½n,
آ آ آ آ And what will he not do to advance his Son?
آ آ آ آ His first-begot we know, and sore have felt,
آ آ آ آ When his fierce thunder drove us to the deep;
آ آ آ آ Who this is we must learn, for man he seems
آ آ آ آ In all his lineaments, though in his face
آ آ آ آ The glimpses of his Fathers glory shine.
آ آ آ آ Ye see our danger on the utmost edge
آ آ آ آ Of hazard, which admits no long debate,
آ آ آ آ But must with something sudden be opposï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Not force, but well couchï؟½t fraud, well woven snares,
آ آ آ آ Eï؟½re in the head of Nations he appear
آ آ آ آ Their King, their Leader, and Supream on Earth.
آ آ آ آ I, when no other durst, sole undertook
آ آ آ آ The dismal expedition to find out
آ آ آ آ And ruine Adam, and the exploit performï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Successfully; a calmer voyage now
آ آ آ آ Will waft me; and the way found prosperous once
آ آ آ آ Induces best to hope of like success.
آ آ آ آ He ended, and his words impression left
آ آ آ آ Of much amazement to thï؟½ infernal Crew,
آ آ آ آ Distracted and surprizï؟½d with deep dismay
آ آ آ آ At these sad tidings; but no time was then
آ آ آ آ For long indulgence to their fears or grief:
آ آ آ آ Unanimous they all commit the care
آ آ آ آ And management of this main enterprize
آ آ آ آ To him their great Dictator, whose attempt
آ آ آ آ At first against mankind so well had thrivï؟½d
آ آ آ آ In Adamï؟½s overthrow, and led thir march
آ آ آ آ From Hellï؟½s deep-vaulted Den to dwell in light,
آ آ آ آ Regents and Potentates, and Kings, yea gods
آ آ آ آ Of many a pleasant Realm and Province wide.
آ آ آ آ So to the Coast of Jordan he directs
آ آ آ آ His easie steps; girded with snaky wiles,
آ آ آ آ Where he might likeliest find this new-declarï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ This man of men, attested Son of God,
آ آ آ آ Temptation and all guile on him to try;
آ آ آ آ So to subvert whom he suspected raisï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To end his Raign on Earth so long enjoyï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ But contrary unweeting he fulfillï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The purposï؟½d Counsel pre-ordainï؟½d and fixt
آ آ آ آ Of the most High, who in full frequence bright
آ آ آ آ Of Angels, thus to Gabriel smiling spake.
آ آ آ آ Gabriel this day by proof thou shalt behold,
آ آ آ آ Thou and all Angels conversant on Earth
آ آ آ آ With man or mens affairs, how I begin
آ آ آ آ To verifie that solemn message late,
آ آ آ آ On which I sent thee to the Virgin pure
آ آ آ آ In Galilee, that she should bear a Son
آ آ آ آ Great in Renown, and callï؟½d the Son of God;
آ آ آ آ Then toldst her doubting how these things could be
آ آ آ آ To her a Virgin, that on her should come
آ آ آ آ The Holy Ghost, and the power of the highest
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½re-shadow her: this man born and now up-grown,
آ آ آ آ To shew him worthy of his birth divine
آ آ آ آ And high prediction, henceforth I expose
آ آ آ آ To Satan; let him tempt and now assay
آ آ آ آ His utmost subtilty, because he boasts
آ آ آ آ And vaunts of his great cunning to the throng
آ آ آ آ Of his Apostasie; he might have learnt
آ آ آ آ Less over-weening, since he failï؟½d in Job,
آ آ آ آ Whose constant perseverance overcame
آ آ آ آ Whateï؟½re his cruel malice could invent.
آ آ آ آ He now shall know I can produce a man
آ آ آ آ Of female Seed, far abler to resist
آ آ آ آ All his sollicitations, and at length
آ آ آ آ All his vast force, and drive him back to Hell,
آ آ آ آ Winning by Conquest what the first man lost
آ آ آ آ By fallacy surprizï؟½d. But first I mean
آ آ آ آ To exercise him in the Wilderness,
آ آ آ آ There he shall first lay down the rudiments
آ آ آ آ Of his great warfare, eï؟½re I send him forth
آ آ آ آ To conquer Sin and Death the two grand foes,
آ آ آ آ By Humiliation and strong Sufferance:
آ آ آ آ His weakness shall oï؟½recome Satanic strength
آ آ آ آ And all the world, and mass of sinful flesh;
آ آ آ آ That all the Angels and ï؟½therial Powers,
آ آ آ آ They now, and men hereafter may discern,
آ آ آ آ From what consummate vertue I have chose
آ آ آ آ This perfect Man, by merit callï؟½d my Son,
آ آ آ آ To earn Salvation for the Sons of men.
آ آ آ آ So spake the Eternal Father, and all Heaven
آ آ آ آ Admiring stood a space, then into Hymns
آ آ آ آ Burst forth, and in Celestial measures movï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Circling the Throne and Singing, while the hand
آ آ آ آ Sung with the voice, and this the argument.
آ آ آ آ Victory and Triumph to the Son of God
آ آ آ آ Now entring his great duel, not of arms,
آ آ آ آ But to vanquish by wisdom hellish wiles.
آ آ آ آ The Father knows the Son; therefore secure
آ آ آ آ Ventures his filial Vertue, though untriï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Against whateï؟½re may tempt, whateï؟½re seduce,
آ آ آ آ Allure, or terrifie, or undermine.
آ آ آ آ Be frustrate all ye stratagems of Hell,
آ آ آ آ And devilish machinations come to nought.
آ آ آ آ So they in Heavï؟½n their Odes and Vigils tunï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ Mean while the Son of God, who yet some days
آ آ آ آ Lodgï؟½d in Bethabara where John baptizï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Musing and much revolving in his brest,
آ آ آ آ How best the mighty work he might begin
آ آ آ آ Of Saviour to mankind, and which way first
آ آ آ آ Publish his God-like office now mature,
آ آ آ آ One day forth walkï؟½d alone, the Spirit leading;
آ آ آ آ And his deep thoughts, the better to converse
آ آ آ آ With solitude, till far from track of men,
آ آ آ آ Thought following thought, and step by step led on,
آ آ آ آ He entred now the bordering Desert wild,
آ آ آ آ And with dark shades and rocks environï؟½d round,
آ آ آ آ His holy Meditations thus persuï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ O what a multitude of thoughts at once
آ آ آ آ Awaknï؟½d in me swarm, while I consider
آ آ آ آ What from within I feel my self, and hear
آ آ آ آ What from without comes often to my ears,
آ آ آ آ Ill sorting with my present state comparï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ When I was yet a child, no childish play
آ آ آ آ To me was pleasing, all my mind was set
آ آ آ آ Serious to learn and know, and thence to do
آ آ آ آ What might be publick good; my self I thought
آ آ آ آ Born to that end, born to promote all truth,
آ آ آ آ All righteous things: therefore above my years,
آ آ آ آ The Law of God I read, and found it sweet,
آ آ آ آ Made it my whole delight, and in it grew
آ آ آ آ To such perfection, that eï؟½re yet my age
آ آ آ آ Had measurï؟½d twice six years, at our great Feast
آ آ آ آ I went into the Temple, there to hear
آ آ آ آ The Teachers of our Law, and to propose
آ آ آ آ What might improve my knowledge or their own;
آ آ آ آ And was admirï؟½d by all, yet this not all
آ آ آ آ To which my Spirit aspirï؟½d, victorious deeds
آ آ آ آ Flamï؟½d in my heart, heroic acts, one while
آ آ آ آ To rescue Israel from the Roman yoke,
آ آ آ آ Then to subdue and quell oï؟½re all the earth
آ آ آ آ Brute violence and proud Tyrannick powï؟½r,
آ آ آ آ Till truth were freed, and equity restorï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ Yet held it more humane, more heavenly first
آ آ آ آ By winning words to conquer willing hearts,
آ آ آ آ And make perswasion do the work of fear;
آ آ آ آ At least to try, and teach the erring Soul
آ آ آ آ Not wilfully mis-doing, but unware
آ آ آ آ Misled; the stubborn only to destroy.
آ آ آ آ These growing thoughts my Mother soon perceiving
آ آ آ آ By words at times cast forth inly rejoycï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And said to me apart, high are thy thoughts
آ آ آ آ O Son, but nourish them and let them soar
آ آ آ آ To what highth sacred vertue and true worth
آ آ آ آ Can raise them, though above example high;
آ آ آ آ By matchless Deeds express thy matchless Sire.
آ آ آ آ For know, thou art no Son of mortal man,
آ آ آ آ Though men esteem thee low of Parentage,
آ آ آ آ Thy Father is the Eternal King, who rules
آ آ آ آ All Heaven and Earth, Angels and Sons of men,
آ آ آ آ A messenger from God fore-told thy birth
آ آ آ آ Conceivï؟½d in me a Virgin, he fore-told
آ آ آ آ Thou shouldst be great and sit on Davidï؟½s Throne,
آ آ آ آ And of thy Kingdom there should be no end.
آ آ آ آ At thy Nativity a glorious Quire
آ آ آ آ Of Angels in the fields of Bethlehem sung
آ آ آ آ To Shepherds watching at their folds by night,
آ آ آ آ And told them the Messiah now was born,
آ آ آ آ Where they might see him, and to thee they came;
آ آ آ آ Directed to the Manger where thou laisï؟½t,
آ آ آ آ For in the Inn was left no better room:
آ آ آ آ A Star, not seen before in Heaven appearing
آ آ آ آ Guided the Wise Men thither from the East,
آ آ آ آ To honour thee with Incense, Myrrh, and Gold,
آ آ آ آ By whose bright course led on they found the place,
آ آ آ آ Affirming it thy Star new gravï؟½n in Heaven,
آ آ آ آ By which they knew thee King of Israel born.
آ آ آ آ Just Simeon and Prophetic Anna, warnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ By Vision, found thee in the Temple, and spake
آ آ آ آ Before the Altar and the vested Priest,
آ آ آ آ Like things of thee to all that present stood.
آ آ آ آ This having heard, strait I again revolvï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The Law and Prophets, searching what was writ
آ آ آ آ Concerning the Messiah, to our Scribes
آ آ آ آ Known partly, and soon found of whom they spake
آ آ آ آ I am; this chiefly, that my way must lie
آ آ آ آ Through many a hard assay even to the death,
آ آ آ آ Eï؟½re I the promisï؟½d Kingdom can attain,
آ آ آ آ Or work Redemption for mankind, whose sins
آ آ آ آ Full weight must be transferrï؟½d upon my head.
آ آ آ آ Yet neither thus disheartnï؟½d or dismayï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ The time prefixt I waited, when behold
آ آ آ آ The Baptist, (of whose birth I oft had heard,
آ آ آ آ Not knew by sight) now come, who was to come
آ آ آ آ Before Messiah and his way prepare.
آ آ آ آ I as all others to his Baptism came,
آ آ آ آ Which I believï؟½d was from above; but he
آ آ آ آ Strait knew me, and with loudest voice proclaimï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Me him (for it was shewï؟½n him so from Heaven)
آ آ آ آ Me him whose Harbinger he was; and first
آ آ آ آ Refusï؟½d on me his Baptism to confer,
آ آ آ آ As much his greater, and was hardly won;
آ آ آ آ But as I rose out of the laving stream,
آ آ آ آ Heaven openï؟½d her eternal doors, from whence
آ آ آ آ The Spirit descended on me like a Dove,
آ آ آ آ And last the sum of all, my Fatherï؟½s voice,
آ آ آ آ Audibly heard from Heavï؟½n, pronouncï؟½d me his,
آ آ آ آ Me his beloved Son, in whom alone
آ آ آ آ He was well pleasï؟½d; by which I knew the time
آ آ آ آ Now full, that I no more should live obscure,
آ آ آ آ But openly begin, as best becomes
آ آ آ آ The Authority which I derivï؟½d from Heaven.
آ آ آ آ And now by some strong motion I am led
آ آ آ آ Into this Wilderness, to what intent
آ آ آ آ I learn not yet, perhaps I need not know;
آ آ آ آ For what concerns my knowledge God reveals.
آ آ آ آ So spake our Morning Star then in his rise,
آ آ آ آ And looking round on every side beheld
آ آ آ آ A pathless Desert, dusk with horrid shades;
آ آ آ آ The way he came not having markï؟½d, return
آ آ آ آ Was difficult, by humane steps untrod;
آ آ آ آ And he still on was led, but with such thoughts
آ آ آ آ Accompanied of things past and to come
آ آ آ آ Lodgï؟½d in his breast, as well might recommend
آ آ آ آ Such Solitude before choicest Society.
آ آ آ آ Full forty days he passï؟½d, whether on hill
آ آ آ آ Sometimes, anon in shady vale, each night
آ آ آ آ Under the covert of some ancient Oak,
آ آ آ آ Or Cedar, to defend him from the dew,
آ آ آ آ Or harbourï؟½d in one Cave, is not revealï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ Nor tasted humane food, nor hunger felt
آ آ آ آ Till those days ended, hungerï؟½d then at last
آ آ آ آ Among wild Beasts: they at his sight grew mild,
آ آ آ آ Nor sleeping him nor waking harmï؟½d, his walk
آ آ آ آ The fiery Serpent fled, and noxious Worm,
آ آ آ آ The Lion and fierce Tiger glarï؟½d aloof.
آ آ آ آ But now an aged man in Rural weeds,

آ آ آ آ (For I discern thee other then thou seemï؟½st)

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis true, I am that Spirit unfortunate,

meaning:
This evocative piece by John Milton represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of John Milton's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
